# ISMS Remediation Roadmap — 26 Weeks to Certification Readiness

**Organisation:** Acme Financial Services Ltd
**Document Ref:** GAP-2024-001-RR
**Based on Assessment:** GAP-2024-001 (2024-11-15)
**Generated:** 2024-11-20
**Generated by:** Marcus Webb, Head of Information Security
**Approved by:** Sarah Chen, CISO
**Board Sponsor:** Chief Executive Officer
**Target Certification Audit:** Q1 2025 (week of 2025-03-10)
**Roadmap Start Date:** 2024-11-25

---

## Overview

This roadmap translates the findings of gap assessment GAP-2024-001 into a structured, prioritised remediation programme. It is organised into four phases, reflecting the logical dependencies between controls and the relative urgency of gaps. Phase 1 addresses the most critical deficiencies that expose the organisation to immediate risk and would result in major non-conformities at audit. Subsequent phases build on the foundation established in Phase 1.

Progress against this roadmap should be reviewed at fortnightly ISMS steering meetings chaired by the CISO. Status updates should be recorded in the ISMS Jira project (project key: ISMS).

**Total estimated effort: 87 person-days across all phases**

---

## Phase 1 — Foundation (Weeks 1–6)

**Priority:** CRITICAL
**Week range:** 2024-11-25 to 2025-01-05
**Objective:** Establish the core policy and governance framework; close the most critical documentation and process gaps identified in GAP-2024-001.

---

### 1.1 Acceptable Use Policy (Control: 5.10)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W1–W2 |
| **Target completion** | 2024-12-08 |
| **Owner** | L. Okonkwo, HR Director |
| **Technical reviewer** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Low — 2 person-days |
| **Priority** | Critical |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 5.10 (Not Implemented) |

**Action description:**
Draft, review and approve an Acceptable Use Policy covering the permitted and prohibited use of Acme information assets, systems, and data. Policy template is available via the `create_policy` tool in iso27001-mcp. The policy must be communicated to all 120 staff with acknowledgement recorded.

**Key deliverables:**
- Acceptable Use Policy document (ISMS-controlled, version 1.0)
- All-staff communication via email (owner: L. Okonkwo)
- Acknowledgement records in HRIS (BambooHR) — 100% completion required

**Dependencies:** None

**Success criteria:** Policy approved, published, and acknowledged by all staff. Evidence uploaded to ISMS evidence register.

---

### 1.2 AWS Configuration Management Baseline (Control: 8.9)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W1–W3 |
| **Target completion** | 2024-12-15 |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Technical reviewer** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 8 person-days |
| **Priority** | Critical |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 8.9 (Not Implemented) |

**Action description:**
Define and implement a formal configuration management baseline for all AWS services in scope (EC2, S3, RDS, IAM, Lambda, API Gateway). AWS Config is already enabled; this task requires defining conformance pack rules, creating a configuration baseline document, and establishing a remediation process for non-conformant resources.

**Key deliverables:**
- Configuration Management Baseline document (ISMS-controlled)
- AWS Config conformance pack deployed with rules covering: S3 public access block, IAM password policy, CloudTrail multi-region enabled, Security Groups unrestricted access, KMS key rotation enabled, MFA on root account
- AWS Config remediation actions configured for critical rules
- Documentation of deviation approval process

**Dependencies:** None

**Success criteria:** AWS Config dashboard showing >95% conformance with baseline rules. Configuration baseline document approved. Evidence screenshots captured.

---

### 1.3 Independent Security Review — Engagement (Control: 5.35)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W2–W4 |
| **Target completion** | 2024-12-20 (engagement confirmed; first review Q1 2025) |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Approver** | CEO |
| **Effort estimate** | High — management overhead; external budget £8,000–£15,000 |
| **Priority** | Critical |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 5.35 (Not Implemented) |

**Action description:**
Formally establish the independent review programme required by ISO 27001:2022 control 5.35. This involves scoping the review, procuring an external auditor (David Hargreaves has already conducted AUDIT-2024-03 and is a suitable candidate), and scheduling the first formal independent review to occur before the certification audit.

**Key deliverables:**
- Statement of Work / engagement letter with external reviewer
- Independent review scope document
- First independent review scheduled for February 2025
- Internal audit programme document (Clause 9.2 requirement)

**Dependencies:** Board approval of budget

**Success criteria:** External reviewer engaged; internal audit programme documented; first review report received before certification audit.

---

### 1.4 Cloud Security Policy (Controls: 5.23, 8.23, 8.26 sub-controls)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W3–W6 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-01-05 |
| **Owner** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Technical reviewers** | J. Patel, Lead Engineer |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 6 person-days |
| **Priority** | Critical |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 5.23 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Draft, review and approve a Cloud Security Policy covering: approved cloud service categories and approval process for new services, configuration standards for AWS (linking to the baseline in 1.2), data residency requirements (UK/EU only), exit criteria and portability requirements, and responsibility allocation between Acme and cloud providers (shared responsibility model).

The policy should address sub-controls 8.23 (web filtering — referencing DNS-layer filtering configuration) and the cloud-specific aspects of 8.26 (application security requirements for cloud-hosted applications).

**Key deliverables:**
- Cloud Security Policy document (ISMS-controlled, version 1.0)
- Cloud service register (approved services list)
- Shared responsibility matrix for AWS services in scope

**Dependencies:** Configuration baseline (1.2) should be drafted first to ensure alignment

**Success criteria:** Policy approved; cloud service register maintained; staff in engineering and IT teams briefed.

---

## Phase 2 — Identity and Access (Weeks 5–12)

**Priority:** HIGH
**Week range:** 2024-12-23 to 2025-02-16
**Objective:** Formalise identity and access management governance across all systems. Close the access-related findings from AUDIT-2024-03 and address related major non-conformities.

---

### 2.1 Identity Management Governance (Control: 5.16)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W5–W7 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-01-12 |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Process input** | L. Okonkwo, HR Director |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 5 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 5.16 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Formalise the identity management governance framework. This includes documenting the full joiner/mover/leaver (JML) process in an ISMS-controlled procedure, establishing an identity register covering all human and service accounts across AWS IAM, GitHub, Jira, and key SaaS applications, and defining account lifecycle SLAs (provisioning within 1 business day; deprovisioning on date of departure).

Integrate with HR system (BambooHR) to trigger JML workflow automatically on relevant HR events.

**Key deliverables:**
- Identity and Access Management Procedure document
- Identity register (spreadsheet or Jira-tracked)
- BambooHR → Jira automation for JML triggers
- JML SLAs formally defined and signed off

**Dependencies:** Access Control Policy (2.2) should be drafted in parallel

**Success criteria:** JML procedure documented; identity register complete; automation tested with 3 example scenarios.

---

### 2.2 Access Control Policy Formal Review (Controls: 5.15, 8.2, 8.3)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W6–W9 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-01-26 |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Technical reviewer** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 4 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Controls 5.15, 8.2, 8.3 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
The existing Access Control Policy (POL-AC-001 v1.0, effective 2024-12-01) covers the high-level principles. This task requires a formal review to update the policy to reflect current cloud-native controls, explicitly link to AWS IAM roles and permission boundaries, and strengthen the privileged access requirements by referencing PAM tooling. The reviewed policy should be approved and re-published as v1.1.

Additionally, document the privileged access rights procedure (8.2) as a sub-document, covering: definition of privileged access, approval process, just-in-time access requirements, and quarterly review cadence.

**Key deliverables:**
- Access Control Policy v1.1 (updated)
- Privileged Access Procedure (new ISMS-controlled document)
- Updated access rights matrix for AWS IAM roles

**Dependencies:** Identity Management Governance (2.1)

**Success criteria:** Policy v1.1 approved; privileged access procedure documented; AWS IAM roles mapped to job functions.

---

### 2.3 MFA Enforcement — Residual Accounts (Control: 5.17)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W7–W10 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-02-02 |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Effort estimate** | Low — 3 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | AUDIT-2024-03 Finding F-003; GAP-2024-001 Control 5.17 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
MFA is currently enforced for 98% of accounts. This task addresses the residual 2%: (a) the legacy service account `svc-build-legacy` identified in AUDIT-2024-03 must be retired or migrated to an IAM role with no interactive login capability; (b) any remaining human accounts without MFA must have MFA enforced via AWS IAM policy condition. Achieve and document 100% MFA coverage.

This task also addresses CAR-003 (raised from AUDIT-2024-03, target 2024-11-01 — already in progress).

**Key deliverables:**
- Legacy service account `svc-build-legacy` retired or migrated (CAR-003 closure evidence)
- AWS IAM policy updated to deny console access without MFA (SCP if multi-account)
- MFA coverage report: 100% of human accounts
- Updated authentication policy section

**Dependencies:** Identity register complete (2.1)

**Success criteria:** Zero human accounts without MFA. Legacy service account decommissioned. CAR-003 closed.

---

### 2.4 Quarterly Access Review Process (Control: 5.18)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W8–W12 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-02-16 |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 6 person-days (including Jira automation setup) |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | AUDIT-2024-03 Finding F-001, CAR-001; GAP-2024-001 Control 5.18 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Implement a formal quarterly access review process to address CAR-001 (major non-conformity from AUDIT-2024-03). The process must cover: all production AWS IAM accounts, GitHub organisation membership, Jira permissions, and key SaaS applications. Jira automation should be configured to create access review tasks quarterly, assign to system owners, and escalate to CISO if not completed within the review window.

The first formal quarterly review (Q4 2024 — already completed as emergency review on 2024-10-05) should serve as the baseline. The Q1 2025 review should be the first fully process-driven review.

**Key deliverables:**
- Access Rights Review Procedure (ISMS-controlled)
- Jira automation configured for quarterly review workflow
- Q1 2025 access review completed and signed off (target: 2025-02-28)
- Escalation rule: overdue reviews auto-escalate to CISO

**Dependencies:** Identity register (2.1); Access Control Policy v1.1 (2.2)

**Success criteria:** CAR-001 evidence package complete; Jira automation active; first automated quarterly review initiated.

---

## Phase 3 — Technical Controls (Weeks 10–20)

**Priority:** MEDIUM / HIGH
**Week range:** 2025-02-02 to 2025-04-13
**Objective:** Implement formalised technical security controls in vulnerability management and secure development, complementing the operational tools already in place.

---

### 3.1 Vulnerability Management Programme (Control: 8.8)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W10–W14 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-03-02 |
| **Owner** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Technical support** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 8 person-days |
| **Priority** | High (CAR-002 in progress — closure by 2024-11-30) |
| **Gap reference** | AUDIT-2024-03 Finding F-002, CAR-002; GAP-2024-001 Control 8.8 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Build on the CAR-002 remediation (already in progress) to formalise the full Vulnerability Management Programme. Tenable.io is operational; this task documents the process, defines SLAs, and establishes governance. CAR-002 closure (2024-11-30) addresses the immediate finding; this phase task completes the programme maturity.

Formalised SLAs:
- **Critical:** Remediate or accept within 24 hours
- **High:** Remediate or accept within 7 calendar days
- **Medium:** Remediate or accept within 30 calendar days
- **Low:** Remediate or accept within 90 calendar days

Tenable.io → Jira integration (already implemented under CAR-002) to create tickets automatically on new findings. Weekly vulnerability review meeting chaired by Marcus Webb.

**Key deliverables:**
- Vulnerability Management Procedure document (ISMS-controlled)
- Tenable.io → Jira integration confirmed operational
- SLA compliance dashboard in Jira
- Weekly vulnerability review meeting cadence established
- Risk acceptance process for findings outside SLA

**Dependencies:** CAR-002 closure evidence (carried forward)

**Success criteria:** Vulnerability procedure approved; zero findings outside SLA without documented risk acceptance; integration operational.

---

### 3.2 Secure Development Lifecycle Policy (Controls: 8.25, 8.31, 8.32)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W12–W16 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-03-16 |
| **Owner** | Lead Engineer |
| **InfoSec reviewer** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | High — 12 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Controls 8.25, 8.31, 8.32 (Partial / Not Implemented) |

**Action description:**
Document and implement a formal Secure Development Lifecycle (SDL) policy and integrate SAST and DAST tooling into the CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions). Current state: branch protection and peer review are in place; no automated security scanning in the pipeline.

**SAST implementation:** Integrate CodeQL (GitHub-native, no additional cost) and Semgrep OSS for custom rules. Configure as required status check on pull requests to main/production branches. Address initial findings before go-live.

**DAST implementation:** Integrate OWASP ZAP (or Burp Suite Enterprise) into the staging deployment pipeline. Run against payment API on each release candidate. Critical/High findings block release.

**SDL policy scope:** Threat modelling requirements (when and how), security requirements definition, secure coding standards, code review requirements, security testing (SAST/DAST), dependency scanning (Dependabot/Snyk), and release approval.

**Key deliverables:**
- Secure Development Lifecycle Policy (ISMS-controlled)
- CodeQL configured as required check in GitHub Actions
- Semgrep OSS with Acme custom ruleset
- DAST scanning integrated into staging pipeline
- Threat modelling template (STRIDE) available for engineering teams
- Separation of environments procedure (Dev → Staging → Production)

**Dependencies:** Configuration baseline (1.2) for environment separation verification

**Success criteria:** SDL policy approved; SAST blocking PRs with new Critical/High findings; DAST running on release candidates; zero Critical SAST findings outstanding.

---

### 3.3 Application Security Requirements (Controls: 8.26, 8.29)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W14–W18 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-03-30 |
| **Owner** | Lead Engineer, with Marcus Webb |
| **Effort estimate** | High — 10 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Controls 8.26, 8.29 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Document formal application security requirements for all systems in scope, with particular focus on the payment processing API. Requirements should be based on OWASP ASVS Level 2 for the payment API and OWASP ASVS Level 1 for internal tooling. Conduct a formal threat modelling exercise for the payment API using STRIDE methodology.

Define security acceptance criteria for new features and releases, and update the Definition of Done in Jira to include security acceptance criteria sign-off.

**Key deliverables:**
- Application Security Requirements document (ISMS-controlled)
- OWASP ASVS mapping for payment processing API
- Threat model for payment API (STRIDE)
- Updated Definition of Done in Jira (security criteria)
- Security testing procedure (8.29) covering SAST, DAST, penetration testing schedule

**Dependencies:** SDL policy (3.2); existing penetration test report (EVD-002)

**Success criteria:** Application security requirements approved; threat model documented; Definition of Done updated across engineering Jira boards.

---

### 3.4 Secure Coding Standards (Control: 8.28)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W15–W20 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-04-13 |
| **Owner** | Lead Engineer |
| **InfoSec reviewer** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 6 person-days |
| **Priority** | Medium |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 8.28 (Partial) |

**Action description:**
Produce a Secure Coding Standards document covering the languages used at Acme (TypeScript/Node.js, Python). The document should reference OWASP Top 10 mitigations, define required cryptographic standards, prohibit known-insecure patterns (e.g., eval(), MD5, hard-coded credentials), and specify input validation and output encoding requirements.

Integrate the standards into the onboarding process for new developers. Add a secure coding checklist to the pull request template in GitHub.

**Key deliverables:**
- Secure Coding Standards document (ISMS-controlled)
- GitHub PR template updated with security checklist
- Developer briefing session conducted (all engineering staff)
- Secure coding section added to security awareness training

**Dependencies:** SDL policy (3.2)

**Success criteria:** Standards document approved; PR template updated; all developers briefed.

---

## Phase 4 — Monitoring and Continuous Improvement (Weeks 18–26)

**Priority:** ONGOING
**Week range:** 2025-03-23 to 2025-05-18
**Objective:** Establish the monitoring, measurement, and management review processes required by ISO 27001:2022 Clauses 9 and 10, and embed continuous improvement into Acme's operational culture.

---

### 4.1 ISMS Performance Evaluation (Control: 9.1)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W18–W22 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-04-13 |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 6 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001 / AUDIT-2024-03 Finding F-006 (Minor NC) |

**Action description:**
Define and implement an ISMS KPI dashboard covering the key performance indicators required to evaluate the effectiveness of the ISMS. KPIs should be reviewed at each fortnightly ISMS steering meeting and presented at the quarterly management review.

**Proposed KPIs:**
- Security awareness training completion rate (target: 100%)
- Mean time to remediate Critical/High vulnerabilities (target: within SLA)
- Access review completion rate (target: 100% quarterly)
- Incidents by severity (trend)
- Open corrective actions by age
- Supplier security reviews completed (target: 100% annually)
- Phishing simulation click rate (target: <5%)

**Key deliverables:**
- ISMS KPI framework document
- Jira dashboard configured with ISMS KPI widgets
- Reporting cadence established (fortnightly steering, quarterly management review)
- Baseline measurements recorded for each KPI

**Dependencies:** Vulnerability management programme (3.1); access review process (2.4)

**Success criteria:** Dashboard operational; first KPI report presented to management; monitoring procedure documented.

---

### 4.2 Security Awareness Training Programme — 100% Completion (Control: 6.3)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W20–W24 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-04-27 |
| **Owner** | L. Okonkwo, HR Director |
| **InfoSec support** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 5 person-days |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 6.3 (Partial — 67% completion) |

**Action description:**
Achieve 100% completion of annual security awareness training (KnowBe4 platform). Implement mandatory completion with line manager escalation for overdue staff. Establish a phishing simulation programme (quarterly) with targeted training for staff who click.

Enhance training content to cover payment security (PCI DSS awareness), remote working security, and social engineering.

**Key deliverables:**
- 100% training completion achieved and evidenced (completion report from KnowBe4)
- Phishing simulation programme established (first simulation by 2025-04-30)
- Training completion tracker in HRIS with line manager visibility
- Annual training calendar published for 2025
- Training acknowledgement records retained as ISMS evidence

**Dependencies:** Acceptable Use Policy (1.1) to be referenced in training content

**Success criteria:** 100% completion evidenced; phishing programme initiated; first simulation completed.

---

### 4.3 Internal Audit Programme (Control: 9.2)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W22–W26 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-05-18 |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Medium — 4 person-days (programme management; audit execution is separate) |
| **Priority** | High |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Control 5.35 / Clause 9.2 (Not Implemented) |

**Action description:**
Establish a formal annual internal audit programme covering all clauses and applicable controls of ISO 27001:2022. AUDIT-2024-03 (conducted September 2024) is the first audit in this programme. Schedule subsequent audits to cover remaining controls by end of 2025. Appoint internal auditors for controls not covered by the external consultant.

**Key deliverables:**
- Internal Audit Programme document (2025 schedule)
- Audit plan template
- Auditor independence matrix (ensuring no self-audit)
- AUDIT-2024-03 formally registered as Audit 1 of the 2024/2025 cycle
- Corrective action tracking confirmed in Jira

**Dependencies:** Independent review engagement (1.3)

**Success criteria:** Audit programme document approved; 2025 audit schedule confirmed; at least one further audit scoped and scheduled.

---

### 4.4 Management Review (Control: 9.3)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Weeks** | W24–W26 |
| **Target completion** | 2025-05-11 |
| **Owner** | Board-level sponsor (CEO) |
| **Secretary** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effort estimate** | Low — 2 person-days (preparation and facilitation) |
| **Priority** | Mandatory (Clause 9.3) |
| **Gap reference** | GAP-2024-001, Clause 9.3 (Not Implemented as formal ISMS review) |

**Action description:**
Conduct the first formal ISMS management review in accordance with Clause 9.3. The review must consider: status of actions from previous reviews, changes affecting the ISMS, ISMS performance (KPIs from 4.1), audit results, risk assessment results, opportunities for continual improvement, and resource requirements.

The management review should be attended by: CEO, CISO, IT Manager, Head of InfoSec, HR Director, DPO, and relevant system owners.

**Key deliverables:**
- Management Review agenda and pack (circulated 1 week in advance)
- Meeting minutes and decisions (ISMS-controlled document)
- Action items with owners and target dates
- Formal sign-off by CEO on ISMS effectiveness and resource commitment

**Dependencies:** KPI dashboard (4.1); audit programme (4.3); corrective actions status

**Success criteria:** Management review conducted and minuted; actions assigned; record retained as ISMS evidence for certification audit.

## Summary Table

| Phase | Focus | Weeks | Controls | Effort (person-days) | Critical Gaps Closed |
|-------|-------|-------|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 — Foundation | Policy and governance | 1–6 | 5.10, 8.9, 5.35, 5.23 | 19 | 4 |
| 2 — Identity & Access | IAM governance | 5–12 | 5.16, 5.15, 5.17, 5.18 | 18 | 1 + 3 CARs |
| 3 — Technical Controls | SecDev and VulnMgmt | 10–20 | 8.8, 8.25, 8.26, 8.28 | 36 | 5 |
| 4 — Monitoring & CI | Clauses 9 and 10 | 18–26 | 6.3, 9.1, 9.2, 9.3 | 17 | 4 |
| **Total** | | **26 weeks** | **16 primary + sub-controls** | **90** | **16** |

---

## Risk to Roadmap

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| Key personnel unavailability (J. Patel, Marcus Webb) | Medium | High | Cross-train backup for each workstream; name deputies at programme kick-off |
| Board sponsor disengagement | Low | High | Monthly executive briefings; link ISMS progress to PCI DSS renewal (board incentive) |
| Certification audit body unavailability in Q1 2025 | Medium | Medium | Book provisional certification audit slot by 2024-12-31 |
| Phase 3 effort underestimated (SDL tooling) | Medium | Medium | Engineering team to validate effort estimate in sprint planning W11 |
| Regulatory intervention (FCA / ICO) diverting resources | Low | High | Accept risk; CISO to maintain brief for regulators on ISMS progress |

*Approved by Sarah Chen, CISO*
